topic: A person finds out their article was published under someone else's name
raw_output:
[story 1]

The email landed in my inbox like a physical blow. My breath hitched, a sharp, shallow intake of air that did little to steady the sudden tremor in my hands. I clicked it open, my eyes darting back and forth across the screen, searching for a misplaced comma, a typo that might signal a mistake. The headline screamed my name, a piece of work I’d poured months of sleepless nights into, but the byline beneath it was utterly foreign. A wave of cold washed over me, making the small hairs on my arms prickle. I reread the attribution, the words blurring slightly. My stomach tightened into a knot, a hard, sickening pressure. I stood up abruptly, pacing the length of my small apartment, the floorboards groaning under my hurried steps. Every shadow seemed to shift, every distant siren sounded like an accusation. I needed to call someone, anyone, but the phone felt impossibly heavy in my grip.

[story 2]

I stared at the screen, my vision tunneling. The words swam before my eyes, the familiar structure of my argument twisted into something alien. A slow, creeping dread began to coil in my chest, making it difficult to draw a full breath. I pressed my palms flat against my eyes, trying to force the text to resolve itself into something truthful, something *mine*. But it wouldn't. The name attached to the piece—a name I didn't recognize, a name that felt borrowed—sent a jolt of pure alarm through me. My jaw clenched so tightly I could feel the muscles strain in my neck. I began to rapidly scroll through the preceding paragraphs, a frantic, desperate sweep, hoping to find the slip-up, the misplaced signature. Each word confirmed the terrifying reality, and a strange, hot flush crept up my neck, leaving me feeling utterly exposed and terribly small in the quiet room.

[story 3]

When I finally read the published piece, the color drained from my face. It wasn't just a realization; it was a systemic shutdown. My fingers hovered over the mouse, paralyzed. The sheer audacity of it—the complete erasure of my contribution—made my throat constrict. I let out a sound that was more a strangled gasp than a word, and I stumbled back from the desk, knocking over a stack of notebooks. The sound of the paper hitting the floor seemed deafening in the sudden silence. I sank onto the edge of my chair, my entire body vibrating with a restless energy. My heart hammered against my ribs, a frantic, uneven rhythm that drowned out all other thought. I kept running my hands through my hair, a desperate, futile attempt to smooth down the chaos inside, unable to focus on anything but the cold, hard fact staring back at me on the monitor.
